summary:
The destruction of the Old Summer Palace (Yuanmingyuan) by British and French forces in October 1860, ordered by the British plenipotentiary Lord Elgin, was one of the most consequential acts of cultural destruction of the nineteenth century and remains a potent symbol in China of Western imperial depredation. The palace complex, built over more than 150 years by successive Qing emperors and covering approximately 350 hectares, was one of the architectural and artistic wonders of the world.

The Yuanmingyuan had been created as a synthesis of Chinese and European baroque garden and palace architecture, containing hundreds of buildings, temples, artificial lakes and mountains, and a vast collection of artworks, porcelains, books, and imperial treasures. Jesuit architects had designed a European baroque section in the eighteenth century, and the palaces contained treasures from across China's thousands of years of civilization.

The destruction came in reprisal for the torture and death of European and Indian prisoners who had been taken during negotiations — 18 Europeans and 14 Indian escorts were killed. Lord Elgin chose to destroy the Summer Palace rather than the Forbidden City itself because he wanted to punish the emperor personally, and because destroying Beijing's central seat of government would have destabilized the entire country. The burning lasted three days; the palace complex was reduced to ruins. British and French soldiers had already looted extensively before the burning began.

The ruins of the Yuanmingyuan's European buildings remain preserved in Beijing as a memorial. The Chinese government has periodically sought the repatriation of looted objects — bronze animal heads from the palace's famous zodiac fountain have been among the most publicized. The destruction is taught in Chinese schools as a defining episode of the 'century of humiliation' and continues to inform Chinese attitudes toward Western powers and their cultural claims.

read_more_links.title: Destruction of the Old Summer Palace – Britannica
